Final Exam Practice Questions

CautionNote

These practice questions are provided to help you prepare for the final exam. Remember, they’re designed to supplement your studying. Please ensure that you’re also reviewing your notes and other course materials (e.g., homeowork assignments).

Mistakes can happen. If you spot any, please report them to the instructor.

Question 1

Use your R skills to examine the data sets pairedDataA.csv and pairedDataB.csv and determine which one actually contains paired data, and which one contains un-paired (independent) data.

Click for Solution 
library(tidyverse)

# The most logical way to go about this is to sort the data and look at it
dataA <- read_csv("data/pairedDataA.csv") |>
  arrange(Subject, Condition)

dataB <- read_csv("data/pairedDataB.csv") |>
  arrange(Subject, Condition)

dataA[1:4, ]
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  Subject Condition    Score
    <dbl> <chr>        <dbl>
1       1 G1        548.8468
2       1 G2        257.2567
3       2 G1        411.6930
4       2 G2        352.5570
dataB[1:4, ]
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  Subject Condition    Score
    <dbl> <chr>        <dbl>
1       1 G1        499.9374
2       2 G1        438.7853
3       3 G1        555.9217
4       4 G1        447.5604

Since A has repeated subjects (i.e., one in each condition), A is paired.



Question 2

Using the dataset you determined contained paired data, examine the assumption, of a classic paired t-test, that the data comes from a normality distributed population. Is the assumption satisfied?

Click for Solution 
# This is paired data, so we need to look at the difference scores
diff <- dataA$Score[dataA$Condition == "G1"] -
  dataA$Score[dataA$Condition == "G2"]

# Plot the differences on a qq plot
ggplot(mapping = aes(sample = diff)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line() +
  xlab("Theoretical Quantiles") +
  ylab("Sample Quantiles")

It’s not bad, but there appears to be a small arcing to the data’s line. If you can use a robust procedure, you probably should.



Question 3

Using the previous question’s data, evaluate the hypothesis that \(H_0: \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 0\) using a classic t-test. Report the test-statistic, degrees of freedom, 95% confidence interval. t.test() is not permitted.

Click for Solution 
# Get Difference scores
diff <-
  dataA$Score[dataA$Condition == "G1"] - dataA$Score[dataA$Condition == "G2"]

# Get Necessary Stats
n <- length(diff)
m_diff <- mean(diff)
s <- sd(diff)
se <- s / sqrt(n)
df <- n - 1

# Test
mu <- 0
t_stat <- (m_diff - mu) / se
p <- pt(t_stat, df = df)

# Confidence interval
alpha <- 0.05
t_crit <- abs(qt(alpha, df = df))
CI <- paste(
  "[",
  round(m_diff - Inf, 3),
  ", ",
  round(m_diff + t_crit * se, 3),
  "]",
  sep = ""
)
cat(
  "T = ",
  round(t_stat, 3),
  "\ndf = ",
  df,
  "\np = ",
  p,
  "\nCI = ",
  CI,
  sep = ""
)
T = 41.278
df = 325
p = 1
CI = [-Inf, 239.208]



Question 4

For the previous question, compute both a Cohen’s d and a Hedge’s g statistic. Which one should you use in this case? Use of cohen.d() is not permitted.

Click for Solution 
# Cohen's d
R <- cor(
  dataA$Score[dataA$Condition == "G1"],
  dataA$Score[dataA$Condition == "G2"]
)

s_rm <- s / sqrt(2 * (1 - R))

d <- m_diff / s_rm

# Hedge's g
g <- d * (1 - (3 / (4 * (n - 1) - 1)))

# Results
cat("d = ", round(d, 3), 
    "\ng = ", round(g, 3))
d =  3.342 
g =  3.335

Since n > 50, Cohen’s d should be used.



Question 5

Create a barplot of the means and the mean difference for the paired data you have been using. Use 95% confidence intervals for the errorbars.

Click for Solution 
# Create a tidy dataframe of the difference scores
Subject <- 1:length(n)
Condition <- rep("Diff", length(n))
Score <- diff
df_diff <- data.frame(Subject, Condition, Score)

# Bind to original data
dataA_diff <- rbind(dataA, df_diff)
# Get group aggregates for plotting
plot_data <- dataA_diff |>
  group_by(Condition) |>
  summarise(
    n = length(Score),
    m = mean(Score),
    se = sd(Score) / sqrt(n),
    alpha = 0.05,
    df = n - 1,
    t_crit = abs(qt(alpha / 2, df = df)),
    moe = t_crit * se
  )

plot_data
# A tibble: 3 × 8
  Condition     n        m       se alpha    df   t_crit       moe
  <chr>     <int>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 Diff        326 230.0158 5.572364  0.05   325 1.967290 10.96246 
2 G1          326 502.9646 4.257322  0.05   325 1.967290  8.375388
3 G2          326 272.9488 3.315021  0.05   325 1.967290  6.521608
# Reorder Factor levels
plot_data$Condition <- factor(plot_data$Condition,
  levels = c("G1", "G2", "Diff")
)

# Plot it
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = Condition, y = m)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    colour = "black",
    aes(fill = Condition)
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = m - moe,
      ymax = m + moe
    ),
    width = 0.25
  ) +
  ylab("Score")



Question 6

Using the paired data, repeat question 3, but examine the hypothesis \(H_0: \mu_1 - \mu_2 > 250\).

Click for Solution 
# Nothing changes from Q3 except mu.
mu <- 250
t_stat <- (m_diff - mu)/se
p <- pt(t_stat, df = df)

# Results
cat("T = ", round(t_stat, 3), 
    "\ndf = ", df, 
    "\np = ", p,
    "\nCI = ", CI,
    sep = ""
    )
T = -3.586
df = 325
p = 0.0001934624
CI = [-Inf, 239.208]



Question 7

Using the paired data, repeat question 6, but use a trimmed t-test. Use 20% trimming.

Click for Solution 
library(WRS2)

# Basic Stats
G <- 0.20
m_diff <- mean(diff, tr = G)
s <- sqrt(winvar(diff, tr = G))

# Degrees of Freedom
h <- n - 2 * floor(G * n)
df <- h - 1

# Test
mu <- 250
t_stat <- ((1 - 2 * G) * (m_diff - mu)) / (s / sqrt(n))
p <- pt(t_stat, df = df)

# Confidence Interval
se <- s / ((1 - 2 * G) * sqrt(n))
alpha <- 0.05
t_crit <- abs(qt(alpha, df = df))
CI <- paste(
  "[",
  round(m_diff - Inf, 3), ", ",
  round(m_diff + t_crit * se, 3),
  "]",
  sep = ""
)

# Results
cat("T  = ", round(t_stat, 3), 
    "\ndf = ", df, 
    "\np  = ", p,
    "\nCI = ", CI,
    sep = ""
    )
T  = -4.556
df = 195
p  = 4.582638e-06
CI = [-Inf, 232.953]



Question 8

Create a barplot of the trimmed means and mean difference for the paired data you have been using. Use 95% confidence intervals for the errorbars.

Click for Solution 
# Get group aggregates for plotting using the dataframe with the difference scores.
plot_data <- dataA_diff |>
  group_by(Condition) |>
  summarise(
    n = length(Score),
    G = 0.20,
    m = mean(Score, tr = G),
    s = sqrt(winvar(Score, tr = G)),
    h = n - 2 * floor(G * n),
    df = h - 1,
    se = s / ((1 - 2 * G) * sqrt(n)),
    alpha = 0.05,
    t_crit = abs(qt(alpha / 2, df = df)),
    moe = t_crit * se
  )

plot_data
# A tibble: 3 × 11
  Condition     n     G        m        s     h    df       se alpha   t_crit
  <chr>     <int> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>
1 Diff        326   0.2 223.2503 63.60035   196   195 5.870830  0.05 1.972204
2 G1          326   0.2 493.2474 54.64181   196   195 5.043883  0.05 1.972204
3 G2          326   0.2 273.0499 39.71956   196   195 3.666438  0.05 1.972204
# ℹ 1 more variable: moe <dbl>
# Reorder Factor levels
plot_data$Condition <- factor(plot_data$Condition,
  levels = c("G1", "G2", "Diff")
)

# Plot it
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = Condition, y = m)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    colour = "black",
    aes(fill = Condition)
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = m - moe,
      ymax = m + moe
    ),
    width = 0.25
  ) +
  ylab("Score")



Question 9

Without using lm(), fit a linear regression model (y~x) to the data in regression.csv. Write out the equation you obtain.

Click for Solution 
data <- read_csv("data/regression.csv")
b1 <- cor(data$x, data$y) * (sd(data$y) / sd(data$x))
b0 <- mean(data$y) - b1 * mean(data$x)

\(\hat{y} = 1324.958 + (-0.997) \cdot x\)



Question 10

Plot the regression data and the line you calculated for it. Do not use geom_smooth().

Click for Solution 
ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(intercept = b0, slope = b1, colour = "red", size = 2)



Question 11

Examine the residuals of the model you made. Do they show heteroscedasticity and non-normality?

Click for Solution 
# Get predicted values
data$y_hat <- b0 + b1 * data$x

# Get Residuals
data$resid <- data$y - data$y_hat

# Look at Residuals
ggplot(data, aes(x = y_hat, y = resid)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 3, colour = "red", size = 1)
# Look at QQ-plot of residuals
ggplot(data, aes(sample = resid)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line() +
  xlab("Theoretical Quantiles") +
  ylab("Sample Quantiles")

It does does not appear to violate either assumption.



Question 12

Run a test to evaluate whether the slope is significantly different than 0. Report the, test statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, and 95% confidence interval. Use of lm() and confint() is not permitted.

Click for Solution 
n <- nrow(data)

# Squared Standard Error of the Residuals
se_resid <- sum(data$resid^2) / (n - 2)

# Sum of squared deviation of X
SSx <- sum((data$x - mean(data$x))^2)

# Standard error of the slope
se_b1 <- sqrt(se_resid / SSx)

# Degrees of Freedom
df <- n - 1 - 1

# Test
B1 <- 0
t_stat <- (b1 - B1) / se_b1
p <- pt(t_stat, df = df) * 2

# Confidence Interval
alpha <- 0.05
t_crit <- abs(qt(alpha / 2, df = df))
CI <- paste(
  "[",
  round(b1 - t_crit * se_b1, 3), ", ",
  round(b1 + t_crit * se_b1, 3),
  "]",
  sep = ""
)

# Results
cat("T = ", round(t_stat, 3), 
    "\ndf = ", df, 
    "\np = ", p,
    "\nCI = ", CI,
    sep = ""
    )
T = -24.769
df = 664
p = 1.981247e-96
CI = [-1.076, -0.918]



Question 13

Examine whether the correlation between x and y for the regression.csv data is significantly different than 0. cor.test() is not permitted. Report the test statistic, degrees of freedom, and p-value.

Click for Solution 
rho <- 0
R <- cor(data$x, data$y)
se <- sqrt((1 - R^2) / (n - 2))

t_stat <- (R - rho) / se
df <- n - 2
p <- pt(abs(t_stat), df = df, lower.tail = FALSE) * 2

# Results
cat("T = ", round(t_stat, 3), 
    "\ndf = ", df, 
    "\np = ", p,
    sep = ""
    )
T = -24.769
df = 664
p = 1.981247e-96



Question 14

Mireault (1990) studied students whose parent had died during their childhood. Among other things, she collected data on their current perceived sense of vulnerability to future loss (PVLoss), their level of social support (SuppTotl). This data can be found in mireault.csv

Fit a ordinary least-squares (OLS) linear regression model that predicts DepressT from the two variables mentioned above. Write out the model equation. You are allowed to use lm().

Click for Solution 
data <- read_csv("data/mireault.csv")

mod_a <- lm(DepressT ~ PVLoss + SuppTotl, data = data)

\(\hat{y} = 58.402 + (0.623) \cdot \text{PVLoss} + (-0.163) \cdot \text{SuppTotl}\)



Question 15

Another variable that Mireault (1990) recorded was the students’ gender. Create a second model that uses gender as a predictor. Conduct a test to determine if the model with gender included provides a significantly better fit than the model without it. Report the F-statistic, degrees of freedom, and p-value. Which model is preferred? You are not permitted to use anova().

Click for Solution 
# Note that Mireault coded gender (a categorical variable) as 1 and 2.

#Factor Gender
data$Gender <- factor(data$Gender)
levels(data$Gender)
[1] "1" "2"
#Set Contrasts
G1_vs_G2 <- c(1, 0)
contrasts(data$Gender) <- G1_vs_G2

#New Model
mod_b <- lm(DepressT ~ PVLoss + SuppTotl + Gender, data = data)

# Note, a different way to do this in R would be to use the ifelse() function.
#Store Summary Stats
mod_A_sum <- summary(mod_a)
mod_B_sum <- summary(mod_b)

#F-statistic
N <- nrow(data)
p_mod2 <- 3
p_diff <- 3 - 2
df1 <- p_diff
df2 <- N - p_mod2 - 1
R_sq_diff <- mod_B_sum$r.squared - mod_A_sum$r.squared

F_stat <- ((N - p_mod2 - 1) / p_diff) * (R_sq_diff / (1 - mod_B_sum$r.squared))

# p-value
p <- pf(F_stat, df1 = df1, df2 = df2, lower.tail = FALSE)

# Results
cat("F = ", round(F_stat, 3), ", df1 = ", df1, ", df2 = ", df2,
      ", p = ", round(p, 3), sep = "")
F = 10.613, df1 = 1, df2 = 97, p = 0.002

Including gender as a predictor significantly improves the model’s fit, making it the preferred model in this case.



Question 16

Create plot of the residuals for the preferred model of the mireault.csv data. Does it violate the assumption of homoscedasticity?

Click for Solution 
ggplot(mapping = aes(x = mod_b$fitted.values, y = mod_b$residuals)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 3) +
  labs(x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals")

No, the residuals appear evenly scattered across the x-axis.



Question 17

Does the preferred model of the mireault.csv data violate the assumption of normally distributed residuals?

Click for Solution 
ggplot(mapping = aes(sample = mod_b$residuals)) +
  stat_qq()

The residuals appear to more or less form a straight line and have no obvious bends (the fitted line is perhaps a bit misleading here). The normality assumption seems satisfied.



Question 18

Is multicolinearity a problem for the preferred model of the mireault.csv data?

Click for Solution 
cor(data[, c("PVLoss", "SuppTotl")])
             PVLoss   SuppTotl
PVLoss   1.00000000 0.05703041
SuppTotl 0.05703041 1.00000000

Given the very low correlation (or variance inflation factor, if you calculated that), multicolinearty does not seem to be an issue.



Question 19

Assume you wanted to run a one-way ANOVA to see if there is at least one significant difference between your levels/groups If you have 7 levels, how big of a sample size would you need to detect a large effect?

Click for Solution 
library(pwr)
pwr_test <- pwr.f2.test(u = 7 - 1, f2 = 0.4^2, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.8)
N <- pwr_test$u + pwr_test$v + 1 # Formal N
N <- ceiling(N / 7) * 7 # Rounded N
N
[1] 98

You would need about 14 observations in each level, which means a total N = 98.



Question 20

The data hemolymph.csv shows the temperature (in degrees Celsius) and the concentration of a mineral in insect hemolymph (in millimoles per liter). Temperature is the independent variable and hemolymph the dependent variable. Plot a line chart of the means with 95% confidence intervals.

Click for Solution 
data <- read_csv("data/hemolymph.csv")

plot_data <- data |>
  group_by(degrees) |>
  summarise(
    n = length(hemolymph),
    GroupMean = mean(hemolymph),
    se = sd(hemolymph) / sqrt(n),
    df = n - 1,
    alpha = 0.05,
    t_crit = qt(alpha / 2, df = df, lower.tail = FALSE),
    moe = t_crit * se
  )

plot_data
# A tibble: 7 × 8
  degrees     n GroupMean        se    df alpha   t_crit       moe
    <dbl> <int>     <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1       3     8  3.474403 0.4052780     7  0.05 2.364624 0.9583301
2       5     8  5.189721 0.2709376     7  0.05 2.364624 0.6406657
3       8     8  6.432857 0.4589317     7  0.05 2.364624 1.085201 
4      14     8  8.021506 0.3949495     7  0.05 2.364624 0.9339071
5      21     8  7.203021 0.2091017     7  0.05 2.364624 0.4944470
6      25     8  6.170271 0.2290908     7  0.05 2.364624 0.5417137
7      28     8  3.942144 0.3386959     7  0.05 2.364624 0.8008886
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = degrees, y = GroupMean)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(colour = "black", size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = GroupMean - moe,
      ymax = GroupMean + moe
    ),
    width = 0.25
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 30, 2)) +
  xlab("Degrees Celsius") +
  ylab("m Hemolymph (mmol/L)")



Question 21

Conduct an omnibus one-way OLS ANOVA F-test on the hemolymph.csv data. Report your F-stat, degrees of freedom, p-value, and \(\omega^2\) effect size. Use of lm() or aov() is not permitted. Is the effect large?

Click for Solution 
# Grand m
grand_m <- mean(data$hemolymph)

# Total Sum of Squares
SST <- sum((data$hemolymph - grand_m)^2)

# Model Sum of Squares
SSM <- sum(plot_data$n * (plot_data$GroupMean - grand_m)^2)

# Residual Sum of Squares
SSR <- SST - SSM

# Degrees of Freedom
df_t <- nrow(data) - 1
df_m <- 7 - 1
df_r <- df_t - df_m

# m Squares
MSm <- SSM / df_m
MSr <- SSR / df_r

# F-Ratio
F_stat <- MSm / MSr

# p-value
p <- pf(F_stat, df1 = df_m, df2 = df_r, lower.tail = FALSE)

# Effect Size
omega_sq <- (SSM - df_m * MSr) / (SSM + SSR + MSr)

# Results
cat("F = ", round(F_stat, 3), ", df1 = ", df_m, ", df2 = ", df_r,
      ", p = ", round(p, 3), ", w^2 = ", round(omega_sq, 3), sep = "")
F = 23.857, df1 = 6, df2 = 49, p = 0, w^2 = 0.71

Yes, the effect size is very large.



Question 22

Given the effect size obtained for the hemolymph.csv data, is the study underpowered? What is Cohen’s \(f^2\) statsitic for the hemolymph.csv data?

Click for Solution 
#Cohen's F^2
R_sq = SSM / SST
cohen_f2 = R_sq / (1 - R_sq)

pwr_test <- pwr.f2.test(u = 7 - 1, f2 = cohen_f2, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.8)
N <- pwr_test$u + pwr_test$v + 1 # Formal N
n_group <- ceiling(N / 7)

cat("Cohen's f_sq = ", round(cohen_f2, 3), sep = "")
cat("\nGroup n = ", n_group, sep = "")
Cohen's f_sq = 2.921
Group n = 2

Assuming an effect size of approx. 2.921 is actually occurring in the population, the study was not even remotely under powered.

  • Note that you can use the function pwr.f2.test to determine how much (post hoc) power the study had given the effect size it obtained by removing the power = argument and inputting the denominator degrees of freedom:

pwr.f2.test(u = 7 - 1, v = 49, f2 = cohen_f2, sig.level = 0.05)

  • Also note that obtaining a large post-hoc power level does NOT mean your study has adequate power. A significant effect will always show high levels of power and you are more likely to get a significant result erroneously if your study lacks adequate power. Thus, significance and post-hoc power are confounded and you always need to consider power prior to running the study (i.e., power needs to be considered a priori).



Question 23

What type of measurement scale is the predictor degrees for the hemolymph.csv data?

Click for Solution


Interval.



Question 24

Conduct a trend analysis on the hemolymph.csv data. State which trend types are significant. What type of trend (as though it isn’t obvious) best describes the data?

Click for Solution 
# Factor Predictor
data$degrees <- factor(data$degrees)

# Set Contrasts
contrasts(data$degrees) <- contr.poly(levels(data$degrees))

# Fit Model to Test
summary(lm(hemolymph ~ degrees, data = data))

Call:
lm(formula = hemolymph ~ degrees, data = data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.90211 -0.68998  0.01345  0.61387  2.21515 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  5.77627    0.12898  44.784  < 2e-16 ***
degrees.L    0.78134    0.34125   2.290   0.0264 *  
degrees.Q   -3.91820    0.34125 -11.482 1.69e-15 ***
degrees.C   -0.52377    0.34125  -1.535   0.1313    
degrees^4    0.36219    0.34125   1.061   0.2937    
degrees^5    0.04325    0.34125   0.127   0.8997    
degrees^6   -0.54726    0.34125  -1.604   0.1152    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.9652 on 49 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.745, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7138 
F-statistic: 23.86 on 6 and 49 DF,  p-value: 5.568e-13

Linear and quadratic are significant, but given the shape of the data, quadratic is clearly the correct choice.



Question 25

Fit the best type of trend line to the hemolymph.csv data and report the formula.

Click for Solution 
# Unfactor
data$degrees <- as.numeric(as.character(data$degrees))

# Create the squared predictor we need for the quadratic equation
data$degrees2 <- data$degrees^2

# Fit the Model
hemoMod <- lm(hemolymph ~ degrees + degrees2, data = data)

\(\hat{y} = (1.297) + (0.865) \cdot x + (-0.027) \cdot x^2\)



Question 26

Plot the trend line from the previous question onto the raw data points.

Click for Solution 
# Get predicted values
x <- seq(0, 30, by = 0.01)

y <- hemoMod$coefficients[1] +
  hemoMod$coefficients[2] * x +
  hemoMod$coefficients[3] * x^2

modPreds <- data.frame(x, y)

# Plot them
ggplot(data, aes(x = degrees, y = hemolymph)) +
  geom_point(
    size = 3, shape = 21,
    fill = "forestgreen",
    colour = "black",
    stroke = 1
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = modPreds, aes(x = x, y = y),
    colour = "red4", linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 30, 2)) +
  labs(x = "Degrees Celsius", y = "m Hemolymph (mmol/L)")



Question 27

Conduct Welch t-tests to see if there are significant pairwise differences between 3, 14, and 28 degrees. Which differences are signifcant according to a Holm-Bonferroni and Benjamini-Hochberg correction? Use of p.adjust() is prohibited.

Click for Solution 
# Subset groups
t3v14 <- filter(data, degrees == 3 | degrees == 14)
t3v28 <- filter(data, degrees == 3 | degrees == 28)
t14v28 <- filter(data, degrees == 14 | degrees == 28)

# Run the tests.
testA <- t.test(hemolymph ~ degrees, data = t3v14)
testB <- t.test(hemolymph ~ degrees, data = t3v28)
testC <- t.test(hemolymph ~ degrees, data = t14v28)

# Get the p-values and store in dataframe for convenience
Test <- c("A", "B", "C")
p <- c(testA$p.value, testB$p.value, testC$p.value)
df <- data.frame(Test, p)
# Holm-Bonferroni---

# Sort dataframe from small to large
df_HB <- df[order(df$p), ]

# Assign Descending Index
df_HB$Index <- 3:1

# Get respective alpha levels
alpha <- 0.05
df_HB$alphaPrime <- alpha / df_HB$Index

# See which values are Significant
# (go from top down and stop when a comparison is no longer significant.)
df_HB$sig <- df_HB$p < df_HB$alphaPrime
df_HB
  Test            p Index alphaPrime   sig
1    A 1.306886e-06     3 0.01666667  TRUE
3    C 2.006764e-06     2 0.02500000  TRUE
2    B 3.912616e-01     1 0.05000000 FALSE
# Benjamini-Hochberg---

# Sort dataframe from small to large
df_BH <- df[order(df$p), ]

# Assign Ascending Index
df_BH$Index <- 1:3

# Get respective alpha levels
alpha <- 0.05
k <- 3
df_BH$alphaPrime <- (df_BH$Index / 3) * alpha

# See which values are Significant
# (go from bottom up and stop when a comparison is significant.)
df_BH$sig <- df_BH$p < df_BH$alphaPrime
df_BH
  Test            p Index alphaPrime   sig
1    A 1.306886e-06     1 0.01666667  TRUE
3    C 2.006764e-06     2 0.03333333  TRUE
2    B 3.912616e-01     3 0.05000000 FALSE

Both the Holm-Bonferroni and Benjamini-Hochberg find a significant differences between 3 v 14 and 14 v 28, but not 3 vs 28.



Question 28

A researcher is interested in whether the effects of cannabis vary with prior usage of the drug. An experiment is conducted in which 12 moderate users, 12 light users, and 12 students who had no prior use of cannabis are randomly sampled from a college population. Within each usage level, half of the subjects are randomly assigned to a placebo condition and the other half to an experimental condition. In the placebo condition, each subject smokes two regular cigarettes that taste and smell like cannabis cigarettes. In the experimental condition, each subject smokes two cannabis cigarettes. Immediately after finishing their cigarettes, each subject is given a reaction time test. The scores (in milliseconds) were recorded. The data for this experiment is found in cannabis.csv.

Plot the means and 95% confidence intervals of the data. Does it look like there is an interaction?

Click for Solution 
data <- read_csv("data/cannabis.csv")

plot_data <- data |>
  group_by(Usage, Condition) |>
  summarise(
    n = length(RT),
    m = mean(RT),
    se = sd(RT) / sqrt(n),
    alpha = 0.05,
    df = n - 1,
    t_crit = abs(qt(alpha / 2, df = df)),
    moe = t_crit * se
  )

plot_data
# A tibble: 6 × 9
# Groups:   Usage [3]
  Usage    Condition     n        m       se alpha    df   t_crit      moe
  <chr>    <chr>     <int>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 Light    Cannabis      6 762      26.38181  0.05     5 2.570582 67.81661
2 Light    Placebo       6 704.8333 28.52066  0.05     5 2.570582 73.31468
3 Moderate Cannabis      6 732.3333 26.67791  0.05     5 2.570582 68.57776
4 Moderate Placebo       6 696      28.93326  0.05     5 2.570582 74.37530
5 None     Cannabis      6 879.1667 22.44017  0.05     5 2.570582 57.68429
6 None     Placebo       6 701.6667 28.01270  0.05     5 2.570582 72.00893
ggplot(plot_data, aes(
  x = Usage, y = m,
  group = Condition, colour = Condition, shape = Condition
)) +
  geom_line(position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = m - moe, ymax = m + moe),
    width = 0.25,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Usage", y = "Reaction Time (ms)")

The lines are not parallel, so there is likely an interaction between Condition and Usage.



Question 29

Using the cannabis.csv. data, test for main effects and an interaction. Report the F ratios, their degrees of freedom, p-value and an \(\omega^2\) measure of effect size. You are not permitted to use lm() or aov().

Click for Solution 
grand_m <- mean(data$RT)
N <- nrow(data)

# Total Sum of Squares
SST <- sum((data$RT - grand_m)^2)
# Model Sum of Squares
sum_stat <- data |>
  group_by(Usage, Condition) |>
  summarise(
    n = length(RT),
    m = mean(RT)
  )

SSM <- sum(sum_stat$n * (sum_stat$m - grand_m)^2)
# Main Effect of Usage: SSA
sum_stat_A <- data |>
  group_by(Usage) |>
  summarise(
    n = length(RT),
    m = mean(RT)
  )

SSA <- sum(sum_stat_A$n * (sum_stat_A$m - grand_m)^2)

# Degrees of Freedom A
dfA <- nrow(sum_stat_A) - 1
# Main Effect of Condition: SSB
sum_stat_B <- data |>
  group_by(Condition) |>
  summarise(
    n = length(RT),
    m = mean(RT)
  )

SSB <- sum(sum_stat_B$n * (sum_stat_B$m - grand_m)^2)

# Degrees of Freedom B
dfB <- nrow(sum_stat_B) - 1
# Interaction between A and B (SSI)
SSI <- SSM - SSA - SSB

# Degrees of Freedom I
dfI <- dfA * dfB
# Residual Sum of Squares (SSR)
SSR <- SST - SSM

# Degrees of Freedom R
A <- length(unique(data$Usage))
B <- length(unique(data$Condition))
dfR <- (N - 1) - (A - 1) - (B - 1) - (A - 1) * (B - 1)
# Mean Squares
MSA <- SSA / dfA
MSB <- SSB / dfB
MSI <- SSI / dfI
MSR <- SSR / dfR
# F-Ratios
FA <- MSA / MSR
FB <- MSB / MSR
FI <- MSI / MSR
# P-Values
pA <- pf(FA, df1 = dfA, df = dfR, lower.tail = FALSE)
pB <- pf(FB, df1 = dfB, df = dfR, lower.tail = FALSE)
pI <- pf(FI, df1 = dfI, df = dfR, lower.tail = FALSE)
# Effect Sizes
omegasqA <- (SSA - (A - 1) * (MSR)) / (SST + MSR)
omegasqB <- (SSB - (B - 1) * (MSR)) / (SST + MSR)
omegasqI <- (SSI - (A - 1) * (B - 1) * (MSR)) / (SST + MSR)
Main Effect of Usage:
    F = 4.341, df1 = 2, df2 = 30, p = 0.02209379, omega^2 = 0.103

Main Effect of Condition:
    F = 16.896, df1 = 1, df2 = 30, p = 0.0002815786, omega^2 = 0.246

Interaction Effect:
    F = 4.008, df1 = 2, df2 = 30, p = 0.02866237, omega^2 = 0.093



Question 30

Given the previous question, describe what the main effects and interactions mean for the cannabis study in plain English and whether they were significant.

Click for Solution

  • There was a significant main effect of usage, meaning that, when Condition is ignored, there was at least one significant difference amoung the levels.

  • There was a significant main effect of Condition, meaning that, when Usage is ignored, there is a significant difference between the Placebo and Cannabis levels.

  • There was a significant interaction between Usage and Condition, meaining that the effect of Usage was significantly different across the Placebo and Cannabis conditions.



Question 31

Recalcualte the two-way ANOVA from the previous question using the lm() function and set planned comparisons to test whether non-users of cannabis differed from users of cannabis, in both the placebo and experimental conditions.

Click for Solution 
# Usage Comparisons
data$Usage <- factor(data$Usage)
levels(data$Usage) # Light, Moderate, None
[1] "Light"    "Moderate" "None"    
# Contrast 1:
LM_v_N <- c(1, 1, -2) / 3

# Contrast 2:
L_v_M <- c(1, -1, 0) / 2

# Set Contrasts
contrasts(data$Usage) <- cbind(LM_v_N, L_v_M)
# Condition Comparisons
data$Condition <- factor(data$Condition)
levels(data$Condition) # "Cannabis" "Placebo"
[1] "Cannabis" "Placebo" 
# Contrast 1:
C_v_P <- c(1, -1) / 2

# Set Contrasts
contrasts(data$Condition) <- cbind(C_v_P)
# The Model
model <- lm(RT ~ Usage + Condition + Usage * Condition, data = data)
summary(model)

Call:
lm(formula = RT ~ Usage + Condition + Usage * Condition, data = data)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-97.333 -50.417   2.833  49.833  95.167 

Coefficients:
                           Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                  746.00      10.99  67.892  < 2e-16 ***
UsageLM_v_N                  -66.62      23.31  -2.858 0.007672 ** 
UsageL_v_M                    19.25      26.92   0.715 0.480008    
ConditionC_v_P                90.33      21.98   4.111 0.000282 ***
UsageLM_v_N:ConditionC_v_P  -130.75      46.62  -2.805 0.008752 ** 
UsageL_v_M:ConditionC_v_P     20.83      53.83   0.387 0.701474    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 65.93 on 30 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5283,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4496 
F-statistic: 6.719 on 5 and 30 DF,  p-value: 0.0002619



Question 32

In plain English, interpret each coefficent from the previous question’s model.

Click for Solution


UsageLM_v_N: Users of cannabis (i.e., subjects in the Light and Moderate groups) showed significantly different reaction times than non-users did (when Condition is ignored). i.e., Non-users had significantly faster reaction times.


UsageL_v_M: There is no significant difference between Light and Moderate users’ reaction time (when Condition is ignored).


ConditionC_v_P: There was a significant difference in reaction time between the Placebo and cannabis conditions (cannabis was slower).

  • Note: because there are only two levels in the factor Condition, this result is just a restatement of the Main Effect of Condition.


UsageLM_v_N:ConditionC_v_P: The significant difference seen between the users and the non-users depended on which Condition was received.


UsageL_v_M:ConditionC_v_P: Condition has no impact on the reaction times seen between the Light and Moderate users.



Question 33

Conduct a simple effects analysis that tests the difference between the Placebo and cannabis conditions at each level of Usage. Which comparisons are significant?

Click for Solution 
# Create a new column with all the level combinations
data$All_levels <- paste(data$Usage, data$Condition, sep = "_")

# Factor the new column
data$All_levels <- factor(data$All_levels)
levels(data$All_levels)
[1] "Light_Cannabis"    "Light_Placebo"     "Moderate_Cannabis"
[4] "Moderate_Placebo"  "None_Cannabis"     "None_Placebo"     
# LM, KP, MM, MP, NM, NP
cont1 <- c(1, 1, 1, 1, -2, -2)
cont2 <- c(1, 1, -1, -1, 0, 0)
cont3 <- c(1, -1, 0, 0, 0, 0)
cont4 <- c(0, 0, 1, -1, 0, 0)
cont5 <- c(0, 0, 0, 0, 1, -1)

grid <- cbind(cont1, cont2, cont3, cont4, cont5)


# Check if each contrast sums to zero
colSums(grid)
cont1 cont2 cont3 cont4 cont5 
    0     0     0     0     0 
# Check if contrasts are orthogonal
t(grid) %*% grid
      cont1 cont2 cont3 cont4 cont5
cont1    12     0     0     0     0
cont2     0     4     0     0     0
cont3     0     0     2     0     0
cont4     0     0     0     2     0
cont5     0     0     0     0     2
# Set Contrasts
contrasts(data$All_levels) <- grid
# contrasts(data$All_levels)
simple <- lm(RT ~ All_levels, data = data)
simple_sum <- summary(simple)
simple_sum

Call:
lm(formula = RT ~ All_levels, data = data)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-97.333 -50.417   2.833  49.833  95.167 

Coefficients:
                Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)      746.000     10.988  67.892  < 2e-16 ***
All_levelscont1  -22.208      7.770  -2.858  0.00767 ** 
All_levelscont2    9.625     13.458   0.715  0.48001    
All_levelscont3   28.583     19.032   1.502  0.14359    
All_levelscont4   18.167     19.032   0.955  0.34744    
All_levelscont5   88.750     19.032   4.663 6.02e-05 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 65.93 on 30 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5283,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4496 
F-statistic: 6.719 on 5 and 30 DF,  p-value: 0.0002619

Only the non non-users showed a significant difference between the two conditions.



Question 34

Zar (1999) presented a dataset that depectied the classification of 250 plants into one of four categories on the basis of seed type (yellow smooth, yellow wrinkled, green smooth, and green wrinkled). Zar used these data to test the null hypothesis that the sample came from a population that had a 9:3:3:1 ratio of these seed types. Test that hypothesis without using chisq.test(). The following code will generate the data:

count <- c(152, 39, 53, 6)
type <- c("YellowSmooth", "YellowWrinkled", "GreenSmooth", "GreenWrinkled")
seeds <- data.frame(type, count)
seeds

Report the test-statistic, degrees of freedom, and p-value. Does the sample likey to come from a population with the hypothesized ratio?

  • Zar, G. H. (1999). Biostatistical methods. Prentice-Hall, New Jersey.
Click for Solution 
# Calculate the expected frequencies:
# The hypothesized ratio of seeds given allows us to calculate the probability of each (expected) seed type.
# We can use those probabilities to determine how many seeds are expected.

# Create a column of probabilities
seeds$probs <- c(9 / 16, 3 / 16, 3 / 16, 1 / 16)

# Expected Frequencies
totalSeeds <- sum(seeds$count)
seeds$expect <- seeds$probs * totalSeeds

# Now run the test:
chiSq <- sum(((seeds$count - seeds$expect)^2) / seeds$expect)

# Degrees of freedom
df <- 4 - 1

# p-value
p <- pchisq(chiSq, df = df, lower.tail = FALSE)
Chi-Sq = 8.972, df = 3, p = 0.0296595

Since p < 0.05, it seems unlikely that the sample came from a population with a 9:3:3:1 ratio.



Question 35

In order to investigate the mortality of Coolibah (Eucalyptus coolibah) trees across Riparian dunes, Roberts (1993) recorded the postion (top, middle, and bottom) of a quadrat along a floodplain and whether it contained dead trees (with and without).

Note: a “quadrat” is marked off square plot of land used in research. In this case, the classification of quadrats according to the presence/absence of dead Coolibah trees will be interpreted as a response variable and the position along the floodplane a predictor variable. The data can be found in “roberts.csv”.

  • Roberts, J. (1993), Regeneration and growth of coolibah, Eucalyptus coolabah subsp. arida, a riparian tree, in the Cooper Creek region of South Australia. Australian Journal of Ecology, (18), 345-350. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1442-9993.1993.tb00461.x

The datafile contains the raw data, create a contingency table using the information it contains. - Hint: there is a stupidly easy way to do this in R that rhymes with “mable”.

Click for Solution 
treeData <- read_csv("data/roberts.csv")

treeTab <- table(treeData$POSITION, treeData$DEAD)
treeTab
        
         With Without
  Bottom   15      13
  Middle    4       8
  Top       0      17



Question 36

Without using chisq.test(), conduct a \(\chi^2\) test to evaluate whether the presense/absence of dead coolibah trees dependent on position. Report the test-statistic, degrees of freedom, and p-value.

Click for Solution 
# This will be simplest if we turn our table into a dataframe with two columns.  R has a convenient way of doing this:
trees <- as.data.frame.matrix(treeTab)
trees <- trees

# Add row names to the dataframe for a nice elegant dataframe; though, this is not strictly necessary.
row.names(trees) <- c("Bottom", "Middle", "Top")
trees
       With Without
Bottom   15      13
Middle    4       8
Top       0      17
# Get Row, Column Totals, and Overall Total
row_total <- apply(trees, MARGIN = 1, FUN = sum)
col_total <- apply(trees, MARGIN = 2, FUN = sum)

# Get Total number of Trees
N <- sum(row_total)

# Get expected Frequencies
## You can do this the long way, or you can use matrix multiplication (i.e., see the last Homework solutions for an explanation).
expected <- row_total %*% t(col_total) / N
# Calculate the test-statistic
chiSq <- sum(((trees - expected)^2) / expected)

# Degrees of Freedom
df <- (3 - 1) * (2 - 1)

# p-value
p <- pchisq(chiSq, df = df, lower.tail = FALSE)
Chi-Sq = 13.66071, df = 2, p = 0.001080472

Since p < 0.05, and the null hypothesis are that the two variables are independent, it seems likely that the presense/absence of dead coolibah trees is dependent on position.



Question 37

What are the odds of finding a dead tree in the middle position?

Click for Solution 
#(With | Middle)
with_mid <- 4 / 8

The odds of finding a dead tree, given that you are looking in the middle position of the quadrat, is 0.5 to 1.



Question 38

What is the probability of finding a dead tree in the middle position?

Click for Solution 
#(With | Middle)
with_mid_pr <- 4 / (4 + 8)

The probability of finding a dead tree, given that you are looking in the middle position of the quadrat is 0.333. i.e., There is a 33.3% chance.



Question 39

What are the odds of finding a dead tree in the top position?

Click for Solution 
#(With | Top)
withTop <- 0 / 17

The odds of finding a dead tree, given that you are looking in the top position of the quadrat is 0 to 1. i.e., based on the observations of Roberts, you are guaranteed to not find a dead tree in the bottom position.

  • Note: One assumes that, in the real world, there probably isn’t a perfect guarantee. If having a guarantee bothers you or you end up with a 0 in the denominator, creating a reality destroying paradox, you can just add a really really small inconsequential value to your numerator and denominator, e.g., 0.000001.
